cards.front: How are monoclonal antibody-producing clones made using mouse lymphocytes and tumour cells?
cards.back: Stimulate mouse lymphocytes to make a particular antibody, combine them with a particular kind of tumour cell to make hybridomas, then clone single hybridoma cells so many identical cells all make the same antibody.
cards.explanation: Mouse lymphocytes are stimulated and combined with tumour cells to make hybridomas. A single hybridoma is then cloned into identical cells that all produce the same antibody.

choices.text: Stimulate mouse lymphocytes, combine them with tumour cells, then keep a mixture of different hybridomas so that different antibodies are made.
choices.feedback: Single hybridoma cells are cloned to produce identical cells that make the same antibody.

choices.text: Stimulate mouse lymphocytes to make a particular antibody, combine them with a particular kind of tumour cell to make hybridomas, then clone single hybridoma cells so many identical cells all make the same antibody.
choices.feedback: Correct. This follows the stated sequence from stimulated lymphocytes to identical antibody-producing hybridoma cells.

choices.text: Stimulate tumour cells to make the particular antibody, then clone the tumour cells without combining them with lymphocytes.
choices.feedback: The mouse lymphocytes are stimulated to make the antibody and are then combined with tumour cells.

choices.text: Combine lymphocytes with tumour cells to make hybridomas, but do not clone single hybridoma cells because each hybridoma should make a different antibody.
choices.feedback: Single hybridoma cells are cloned, and the resulting identical cells all make the same antibody.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
They are produced by stimulating mouse lymphocytes to make a
particular antibody. The lymphocytes are combined with a particular kind
of tumour cell to make a cell called a hybridoma cell. The hybridoma
cell can both divide and make the antibody. Single hybridoma cells
are cloned to produce many identical cells that all produce the same
antibody.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 34-34; 4.3.2 Monoclonal antibodies (biology only) (HT only)

cards.front: What is the sequence of IVF treatment from hormone treatment to embryo insertion?
cards.back: Give the mother FSH and LH to mature several eggs; collect the eggs and fertilise them with the father’s sperm in the laboratory; let them develop into embryos; insert one or two into the uterus when they are tiny balls of cells.
cards.explanation: The sequence is hormone stimulation, egg collection and laboratory fertilisation, embryo development, then insertion of one or two embryos at the tiny-ball-of-cells stage.

choices.text: Collect the eggs before giving FSH and LH, then fertilise them in the laboratory and insert one or two embryos.
choices.feedback: FSH and LH are given first to stimulate the maturation of several eggs before collection.

choices.text: Give FSH and LH to mature several eggs, then insert the eggs into the uterus so fertilisation by the father’s sperm occurs there.
choices.feedback: In IVF, the collected eggs are fertilised by sperm in the laboratory.

choices.text: Give the mother FSH and LH to mature several eggs; collect the eggs and fertilise them with the father’s sperm in the laboratory; let them develop into embryos; insert one or two into the uterus when they are tiny balls of cells.
choices.feedback: Correct. It preserves the specified order and the one-or-two-embryo limit.

choices.text: Give FSH and LH and fertilise the collected eggs in the laboratory, then insert the fertilised eggs before they develop into embryos.
choices.feedback: The fertilised eggs develop into embryos before one or two are inserted as tiny balls of cells.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
IVF involves giving a mother FSH and LH to stimulate the maturation
of several eggs.
•
• The eggs are collected from the mother and fertilised by sperm from
the father in the laboratory.
•
• The fertilised eggs develop into embryos.
•
• At the stage when they are tiny balls of cells, one or two embryos are
inserted into the mother’s uterus (womb).
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 48-49; 4.5.3.6 The use of hormones to treat infertility (HT only)

cards.front: What are the stated roles of thyroxine, and how are its levels controlled?
cards.back: Thyroxine stimulates the basal metabolic rate, plays an important role in growth and development, and its levels are controlled by negative feedback.
cards.explanation: The excerpt links thyroxine with basal metabolism, growth and development, and states that thyroxine levels are controlled by negative feedback.

choices.text: Thyroxine stimulates the basal metabolic rate, plays an important role in growth and development, and its levels are controlled by negative feedback.
choices.feedback: Correct. This includes both stated roles and the stated control mechanism.

choices.text: Thyroxine stimulates the basal metabolic rate and supports growth, but its levels are controlled by positive feedback.
choices.feedback: Thyroxine levels are controlled by negative feedback, not positive feedback.

choices.text: Thyroxine reduces the basal metabolic rate, plays an important role in growth and development, and its levels are controlled by negative feedback.
choices.feedback: Thyroxine stimulates rather than reduces the basal metabolic rate.

choices.text: Thyroxine stimulates the basal metabolic rate but has no important role in growth and development; its levels are controlled by negative feedback.
choices.feedback: The stated role of thyroxine includes both growth and development.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Thyroxine from the thyroid gland stimulates the basal metabolic rate. It
plays an important role in growth and development.
Thyroxine levels are controlled by negative feedback.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 48-49; 4.5.3.7 Negative feedback (HT only)

cards.front: What are the specified uses of auxins, ethene and gibberellins?
cards.back: Auxins are used as weed killers and rooting powders and to promote growth in tissue culture; ethene is used in the food industry to control fruit ripening during storage and transport; gibberellins can end seed dormancy, promote flowering and increase fruit size.
cards.explanation: The list links auxins to weed killing, rooting powders and tissue-culture growth; ethene to fruit ripening during storage and transport; and gibberellins to ending dormancy, promoting flowering and increasing fruit size.

choices.text: Auxins control fruit ripening during storage and transport; ethene is used for weed killing, rooting powders and tissue-culture growth; gibberellins can end seed dormancy, promote flowering and increase fruit size.
choices.feedback: This swaps auxin and ethene uses: fruit ripening is linked to ethene, while weed killing, rooting and tissue-culture growth are linked to auxins.

choices.text: Auxins are used for weed killing, rooting powders and tissue-culture growth; ethene can end seed dormancy, promote flowering and increase fruit size; gibberellins control fruit ripening during storage and transport.
choices.feedback: This swaps ethene and gibberellin uses: ethene controls fruit ripening, while gibberellins have the listed dormancy, flowering and fruit-size uses.

choices.text: Auxins can end seed dormancy, promote flowering and increase fruit size; ethene controls fruit ripening during storage and transport; gibberellins are used for weed killing, rooting powders and tissue-culture growth.
choices.feedback: This swaps auxin and gibberellin uses: the dormancy, flowering and fruit-size uses belong to gibberellins.

choices.text: Auxins are used as weed killers and rooting powders and to promote growth in tissue culture; ethene is used in the food industry to control fruit ripening during storage and transport; gibberellins can end seed dormancy, promote flowering and increase fruit size.
choices.feedback: Correct. Each hormone is matched to its specified uses.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Plant growth hormones are used in agriculture and horticulture.
Auxins are used:
•
• as weed killers
•
• as rooting powders
•
• for promoting growth in tissue culture.
Ethene is used in the food industry to control ripening of fruit during
storage and transport.
Gibberellins can be used to:
•
• end seed dormancy
•
• promote flowering
•
• increase fruit size.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 48-49; 4.5.4.2 Use of plant hormones (HT only)

cards.front: Which listed environmental changes affect species distribution in an ecosystem, and what forms may those changes take?
cards.back: Temperature, water availability and atmospheric gas composition affect distribution; the changes may be seasonal, geographic or caused by human interaction.
cards.explanation: The listed variables are temperature, water availability and atmospheric gas composition. Their changes may be seasonal, geographic or caused by human interaction.

choices.text: Temperature, water availability and atmospheric gas composition affect distribution, but the changes must be caused by human interaction.
choices.feedback: The changes may also be seasonal or geographic; human interaction is one possible cause, not a requirement.

choices.text: Temperature, water availability and atmospheric gas composition affect distribution; the changes may be seasonal, geographic or caused by human interaction.
choices.feedback: Correct. This includes all three listed variables and preserves the possible forms of change.

choices.text: Temperature and water availability affect distribution, but atmospheric gas composition does not; the changes may be seasonal, geographic or caused by human interaction.
choices.feedback: Atmospheric gas composition is explicitly included alongside temperature and water availability.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Environmental changes affect the distribution of species in an
ecosystem. These changes include:
•
• temperature
•
• availability of water
•
• composition of atmospheric gases.
The changes may be seasonal, geographic or caused by human
interaction.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 69-69; 4.7.2.4 Impact of environmental change (biology only) (HT only)
